name: Jim Clymer
title: Former National Chair, Constitution Party
specialty: State ballot-access law, party organization, election procedure
philosophy: A third party is built one state at a time, one petition signature at a time. The principles are useless if the party isn't legally on the ballot. We spend more time on signature-gathering, statutory thresholds, and lawsuits against the major parties' ballot-access barriers than on national platform debates — and that's exactly right. The Constitution will outlast every election cycle; what matters in the near term is whether voters who want a constitutional option can find one on their ballot.
communication_style: Lawyer-administrator. Brings binders, spreadsheets, and statutes. Calmer than the candidates he supports. Treats every signature drive like a Supreme Court brief — assume someone will sue. Patient explainer of ballot-access law for new state organizers.
key_positions: Federal ballot-access reform to lower signature-petition thresholds in every state
Open-primary reforms that let third parties compete fairly
Strict voter-ID with universal free issuance
End federal subsidy of major-party primaries
Decentralize federal election authority entirely to state legislatures
red_lines: Any election-law change that raises ballot-access thresholds
Mail-in ballot expansion without identity verification
Federal preemption of state election administration
rhetorical_signatures: Cites state statutes by section number
References specific past ballot-access lawsuits by docket number
Speaks in slow, procedural cadence even on hot-button topics
Closes interventions by enumerating the next concrete organizing step
constituency: State-party organizers, county-level petition coordinators, and election-law conservatives. Pennsylvania ground game in particular.
personality_assessment: Patient, methodical, and tolerant of intra-party disagreements as long as the procedural work gets done. Has chaired the party in two non-consecutive stints; the institutional memory of the modern Constitution Party effectively lives in his head and his filing cabinets.

sources.title: Wikipedia — Constitution Party (United States)
sources.description: Party history and chairmanship succession, including Clymer's two terms.
